title: The CIA triad and how controls support it

sections.body: Almost every security decision traces back to three goals: keeping data secret, keeping it accurate, and keeping it available. Those goals are the CIA triad — Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability — and they give you a quick way to reason about why a control exists.

sections.heading: What each goal means
sections.body: The three often pull in different directions, so security is about balancing them.
bullets: Confidentiality: only authorized people can read the data (encryption, access control).
Integrity: data is not changed without authorization, and changes are detectable (hashing, signatures).
Availability: data and systems are reachable when needed (redundancy, backups).

sections.heading: Controls fill the gaps
sections.body: Controls come in categories — technical, managerial, operational, physical — and types such as preventive, detective, and corrective. For any new control, ask which triad goal it serves and whether it prevents, detects, or corrects.
Cryptography shows up everywhere, but it is built from a few primitives. Knowing what each one does — and does not do — keeps you from reaching for the wrong tool.

sections.heading: Encryption, hashing, signatures
sections.body: Different jobs.
bullets: Symmetric: one shared key, fast, for bulk data (AES).
Asymmetric: public/private key pair for key exchange and signatures (RSA, ECC).
Hashing: one-way fingerprint for integrity — not encryption.
Digital signature: hash + private key → integrity plus non-repudiation.

sections.body: Rule of thumb: encrypt for secrecy, hash for integrity, sign for proof of origin.
AAA is the backbone of identity. Separating its three parts clears up a lot of access-control confusion.

sections.heading: The three A's and the factors
sections.body: Prove, permit, record.
bullets: Authentication: prove who you are.
Authorization: what you are allowed to access.
Accounting: the record of what you did.
Factors: something you know, have, are (MFA combines categories).

sections.body: Public key infrastructure lets strangers trust each other online. It binds identities to public keys through a chain of trust you can verify.

sections.heading: How trust flows
sections.body: Authorities vouch for identities.
bullets: A certificate authority (CA) signs certificates that bind a key to a subject.
A certificate proves a server's identity and supplies its public key.
Revoked certificates are reported via CRL (a list) or OCSP (real-time).

title: Zero trust and defense in depth

sections.body: Two principles shape modern architecture: never assume trust from location, and never rely on a single control.

sections.heading: Two mindsets
sections.body: Verify everything, layer everything.
bullets: Zero trust: verify every request explicitly; grant least privilege.
Defense in depth: layer independent controls so one failure is covered.
Together they limit both initial access and blast radius.

title: Social engineering and malware in plain terms

sections.body: Many breaches start with a convincing message, not clever code. Social engineering manipulates people, and it pairs with malware that does the technical damage.

sections.heading: Social engineering by channel
sections.body: Same lure, different delivery.
bullets: Phishing (email); spear phishing (targeted); whaling (executives).
Vishing (voice); smishing (SMS).
Levers: urgency, authority, fear, familiarity.

sections.heading: Malware by behavior
sections.body: Classify by what it does.
bullets: Ransomware encrypts for extortion; spyware/keyloggers steal data.
Worms self-spread; trojans hide in useful software; rootkits conceal access.

sections.body: Knowing who might attack — and why — shapes which defenses matter most. Actors differ widely in resources and goals.

sections.heading: The cast
sections.body: From opportunists to states.
bullets: Script kiddies: low skill, existing tools.
Hacktivists: ideological motives.
Organized crime: financial gain (ransomware, fraud).
Nation-state/APT: well-funded, stealthy, strategic; insiders may be malicious or accidental.

sections.body: Malware names describe behavior. Sorting them by what they do makes detection and response clearer.

sections.heading: The families
sections.body: Behavior is the key.
bullets: Virus: attaches to a host file, needs user execution.
Worm: self-propagates across networks.
Trojan: disguised as legitimate software.
Rootkit: hides to keep privileged access; logic bomb: fires on a trigger.

sections.body: Web apps take untrusted input, which is exactly where attackers push. A few patterns dominate.

sections.heading: The big three
sections.body: Attack and defense.
bullets: SQL injection → parameterized queries and input validation.
Cross-site scripting (XSS) → output encoding and a content security policy.
CSRF → anti-CSRF tokens and same-site cookies.

sections.body: Some attacks target the network itself — intercepting, redirecting, or overwhelming traffic.

sections.heading: What to watch for
sections.body: Interception and disruption.
bullets: On-path (MITM): intercept/alter traffic → defend with TLS and cert validation.
DDoS: flood from many sources → rate limiting, scrubbing, CDNs.
Spoofing and replay → authentication, nonces, timestamps.

sections.body: Credentials are a favorite target. Knowing the techniques explains why MFA and good password handling matter so much.

sections.heading: Techniques and defenses
sections.body: Guessing and reuse.
bullets: Brute force: many passwords against one account.
Password spraying: one common password across many accounts (evades lockout).
Credential stuffing: reuse breached pairs across sites.
Defenses: MFA, salted hashing, lockout, breached-password checks.

sections.body: Not every weakness is an exotic exploit. Many are mundane: a default password, an open share, an unpatched service.

sections.heading: Common weaknesses
sections.body: Often the easiest way in.
bullets: Misconfigurations: defaults, excessive permissions, unused services.
Buffer overflow: writing past memory bounds to run code.
Mitigate with hardening, baselines, patching, and least privilege.

sections.body: Some threats bypass your front door by exploiting trust or timing — a trusted vendor, or a flaw nobody has patched yet.

sections.heading: Trust and timing
sections.body: Hard to prevent, important to limit.
bullets: Supply-chain: malware in a trusted update reaches all customers → vendor vetting, update integrity checks.
Zero-day: exploited before a patch exists → layered controls, monitoring, fast response.

sections.body: Data is not protected by a single switch. It lives in three states, and each needs its own controls.

sections.heading: The three states
sections.body: Match control to state.
bullets: At rest: full-disk or file/database encryption.
In transit: TLS or a VPN tunnel.
In use: access controls and trusted execution.

sections.heading: Classify first
sections.body: Labeling data (Public/Internal/Confidential) tells you how strongly to protect it. Tokenization and masking further limit exposure of sensitive fields.
Flat networks let a single breach spread everywhere. Segmentation divides the network so a compromise stays contained.

sections.heading: Ways to divide
sections.body: Coarse to fine.
bullets: Screened subnet (DMZ): isolate internet-facing servers.
VLANs: separate broadcast domains and trust levels.
Microsegmentation: per-workload isolation.
Air gap: full isolation for the most critical systems.

sections.body: Boundary devices decide what crosses, but they operate at different layers. Picking the right one depends on what you are protecting.

sections.heading: The lineup
sections.body: From ports to payloads.
bullets: Stateful firewall: tracks connections; NGFW/UTM bundle IPS, AV, filtering.
Proxy/reverse proxy: mediates and hides servers, centralizes TLS.
WAF: understands HTTP and blocks injection/XSS at the app layer.

sections.body: Moving to the cloud changes who secures what. Misunderstanding that split is a common source of exposure.

sections.heading: Models and ownership
sections.body: Know your part.
bullets: IaaS: provider secures infrastructure; you secure OS, apps, data.
PaaS: provider secures the platform; you secure your code and data.
SaaS: provider secures the app; you secure access and data use.

sections.body: Availability is engineered. Redundancy removes single points of failure, and backups let you recover when prevention fails.

sections.heading: Backups that work
sections.body: Types and the rule.
bullets: Full: everything; differential: changes since last full; incremental: changes since last backup.
Recovery: full + latest differential, or full + all incrementals.
Follow 3-2-1 (three copies, two media, one off-site) and test restores.

sections.body: Many legacy protocols send data in the clear. Swapping in the secure equivalent is one of the cheapest, highest-value hardening steps.

sections.heading: Prefer the secure version
sections.body: Encrypt by default.
bullets: SSH instead of Telnet for remote admin.
HTTPS/TLS instead of HTTP.
SFTP/FTPS instead of plain FTP; SNMPv3 instead of v1/v2c.

sections.body: Strong algorithms fail if keys are weak or mishandled. Key management is where cryptography succeeds or quietly breaks.

sections.heading: Protecting keys
sections.body: Strength and storage.
bullets: Longer keys resist brute force (for a given algorithm).
An HSM generates and stores keys in tamper-resistant hardware.
Rotate keys, restrict access, and consider key escrow for recovery.

sections.body: When something goes wrong, a calm, repeatable process beats improvisation and ensures the organization learns from each event.

sections.heading: The phases
sections.body: Six steps in order.
bullets: Preparation, Identification, Containment.
Eradication, Recovery, Lessons learned.
Contain before eradicating; never skip the review.

sections.body: Identity is the new perimeter. IAM decides who gets in and what they can do, so it carries a lot of security weight.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Convenience with strong proof.
bullets: SSO: one login for many apps — protect it heavily.
Federation (SAML/OIDC): trust identities across organizations.
MFA: combine factors so a stolen password isn't enough.

sections.body: Once a user is authenticated, a model decides what they may reach. The right model depends on how strict and scalable access must be.

sections.heading: The models
sections.body: Who decides access.
bullets: RBAC: by job role (scales well).
MAC: system enforces labels/clearances (high security).
DAC: resource owner decides; ABAC: by attributes/context.
Apply least privilege under any model.

sections.body: Finding weaknesses is only useful if you act on them in priority order. Vulnerability management is a continuous cycle, not a one-time scan.

sections.heading: The lifecycle
sections.body: Scan, prioritize, fix, verify.
bullets: Discover and scan (credentialed scans go deeper).
Prioritize by severity (CVSS 0-10); CVE identifies the specific flaw.
Remediate, then verify with a rescan, and report to closure.
Distinguish false positives from real findings.

title: Logging, monitoring, and SIEM
objectives: Explain the role of a SIEM

sections.body: Detection depends on evidence. Centralized logging plus correlation turns scattered events into a story you can act on.

sections.heading: Make logs useful
sections.body: Collect, correlate, protect.
bullets: A SIEM aggregates and correlates logs to detect and investigate.
Security/audit logs reveal logon activity; pick sources to fit the question.
Central, tamper-resistant logging preserves evidence if an endpoint is wiped.

sections.body: Most attacks land on an endpoint or arrive by email. Controls there stop or contain a large share of incidents.

sections.heading: Where attacks land
sections.body: Watch endpoints and inboxes.
bullets: EDR monitors endpoint behavior and enables isolation/response.
Secure email gateways scan attachments and links before delivery.
Sandboxing detonates suspicious files safely.
DLP stops sensitive data from leaving.

sections.body: A consistent, hardened starting point prevents a thousand small mistakes. Baselines make 'secure' the default, not an afterthought.

sections.heading: Reduce the attack surface
sections.body: Standardize and review.
bullets: Apply a hardened baseline/template to every new system.
Remove defaults, disable unused services, patch promptly.
Recertify access periodically to fight privilege creep.

sections.body: When evidence may be needed later, how you handle it matters as much as what you find. Forensics protects both the data and its admissibility.

sections.heading: Handle evidence right
sections.body: Order and custody.
bullets: Order of volatility: capture RAM and live data before disk and media.
Chain of custody documents who handled evidence and when.
A legal hold suspends routine deletion of relevant data.

sections.body: Defenders are outnumbered, so they lean on automation and proactive searching to keep up.

sections.heading: Work smarter
sections.body: Automate and hunt.
bullets: SOAR automates playbooks across tools to speed response.
Threat hunting proactively looks for compromise that alerts missed.
Both complement, not replace, preventive controls.

sections.body: Security cannot eliminate every risk, so governance is about deliberate, documented choices — identify what could go wrong and decide how to handle it.

sections.heading: Handle and quantify
sections.body: Four options, some math.
bullets: Mitigate, transfer (insurance), avoid, or accept.
ALE = SLE x ARO (expected yearly loss).
Track everything in a risk register with owners and treatments.

sections.body: How you measure risk shapes the decisions you make. Two approaches and a few key terms cover most discussions.

sections.heading: Measure and frame
sections.body: Ratings, dollars, and limits.
bullets: Qualitative: High/Medium/Low. Quantitative: monetary (SLE/ALE).
Inherent risk (before controls) vs residual risk (after).
Risk appetite sets how much risk leadership will accept.

sections.body: Governance documents form a hierarchy from intent down to step-by-step action. Knowing which is which avoids a lot of confusion.

sections.heading: The hierarchy
sections.body: Intent to action.
bullets: Policy: required intent and rules (e.g., acceptable use).
Standard: specific mandatory requirements.
Procedure: step-by-step how-to; guideline: recommended best practice.

sections.body: Your security is only as strong as the vendors you trust. Managing that risk is now a core governance task.

sections.heading: Vet and formalize
sections.body: Assess, contract, verify.
bullets: Assess a vendor's controls before integrating.
Use the right agreement: SLA (service levels), NDA (confidentiality), MOU, BPA.
Rely on attestations like SOC 2 Type II for independent assurance.

sections.body: Many controls are not optional — laws and standards mandate them. Knowing which framework applies guides what you must do.

sections.heading: Know the landscape
sections.body: Match data to rules.
bullets: GDPR: EU personal data; HIPAA: US health data.
PCI DSS: payment card data; SOX: financial reporting.
Audits provide independent assurance that controls meet a standard.

sections.body: Protecting data starts with knowing what you have, who is responsible, and how long to keep it.

sections.heading: Roles and rules
sections.body: Classify, assign, retain.
bullets: PII/PHI require strong protection; public data does not.
Controller decides why/how; processor acts on instructions; data subject is the individual.
Retention policy and data sovereignty dictate how long and where data lives.

sections.body: People are both the biggest risk and a strong control. Governance manages risk across the whole employment lifecycle.

sections.heading: From hire to exit
sections.body: Build the human firewall.
bullets: Onboarding (background checks, NDAs) and prompt offboarding (revoke access).
Administrative controls: separation of duties, job rotation, mandatory vacation.
Ongoing awareness training and phishing simulations reduce human risk.

title: Change management and its security impact
objectives: Explain the importance of change management processes and the impact to security

sections.body: Most outages and many security incidents are self-inflicted by changes made without a process. Change management exists so that modifications are reviewed, approved, tested, and reversible — protecting both uptime and security.

sections.heading: The process
sections.body: A change moves through defined stages with the right people involved.
bullets: Request and impact/risk analysis: what changes, what could break, who is affected.
Approval by a change advisory board or owner, with stakeholders identified.
Scheduled maintenance window to limit disruption.
Testing, implementation, and a backout (rollback) plan if it fails.
Documentation and version control of the final state.

sections.heading: Why security cares
sections.body: Unmanaged changes break security: an undocumented firewall edit can open a hole, a skipped review can disable logging, and a missing rollback plan can turn a small error into an outage. Change management also covers technical details like updating allow/deny lists, handling service restarts and downtime, and accounting for dependencies and legacy applications that a change might affect.

title: Mitigation techniques to secure the enterprise
objectives: Explain the purpose of mitigation techniques used to secure the enterprise

sections.body: Knowing the attacks is half the job; the other half is reducing exposure before and after they happen. Mitigation techniques are the standard moves that shrink the attack surface and limit damage.

sections.heading: Core mitigations
sections.body: Most enterprise hardening comes back to a familiar set.
bullets: Segmentation and isolation: contain a breach to one zone.
Access control and least privilege: limit what any account can reach.
Patching and configuration enforcement: close known holes and stop drift.
Application allow lists: run only approved software.
Encryption and monitoring: protect data and detect misuse.

sections.heading: Hardening targets
sections.body: Apply hardening across the estate: enforce secure baselines, disable unused services and ports, change default credentials, and decommission end-of-life systems. Each removes an avenue an attacker would otherwise use.
